title: The Calling of Matthew
subtitle: Christ summons a tax-collector from his money-table, and feasts in his house

stillness: Behold, I stand at the door and knock;
if any man open to me, I will come in to him,
and will sup with him, and he with me.
Make ready, in the quiet, the house that is your heart.
gospel: And as the Lord Jesus passed on,
he came to the sea of Galilee,
and saw a man sitting at the tax-table,
Matthew, who before his calling was named Levi, the son of Alphaeus.
And he said to him: Follow me.
And immediately, rising up, he left all things,
and followed him, without any delay.
And Levi made him a great feast in his own house.
And behold, many publicans and sinners came,
and sat down with Jesus and his disciples.
But the Scribes and Pharisees murmured,
Why doth your Master eat and drink with publicans and sinners?
I came not to call the just, but sinners.
behold: Attend to each thing, as if you were present.
Behold the Lord Jesus, passing by the sea of Galilee,
looking upon Matthew — not so much with the eye of the body
as with the gaze of the mind, and of one who has pity.
He sits at the tax-table, gripping at gain with a stubborn heart;
for to sit there marks his lingering, his clinging to profit.
See the very splendour and majesty of the hidden Godhead,
which shone even in his human face,
able to draw to itself, at the first glance, those who beheld him.
If the lodestone has this power, to draw iron,
how much more the Lord of all creatures, to draw whom he willed?
Behold Matthew: he does not resist, he does not waver,
but at once he obeys — rising, leaving all, following.
He cuts the rope, he does not untie it.
consider: Christ called Matthew, busied with such base things,
that no one might despair of the grace of God,
however vile the work that has held him.
He left even his own goods, who was wont to seize what was another's;
for the tax-table, says Chrysostom, is robbery made lawful.
And as the outward voice called, within he kindled, drew, and taught the way.
Joyful and grateful, Matthew receives the Lord into his house,
and sets a great feast before him —
great, from the great devotion and delight of his mind,
so great that from its leavings the Angels are fed in heaven;
for there is joy among the Angels of God over one sinner doing penance.
His fellow publicans came to him as to a comrade of the same craft;
and other sinners, now repenting, came hoping for pardon.
Seeing the publican converted and finding place for penance,
they too did not despair, but came as penitents to recline with him —
that he might have for companions in penance those he had in guilt.
But the Pharisees, clinging to a narrow justice and turning from mercy,
murmured. They erred doubly:
they thought themselves just, while they were proud;
and these others guilty, while already they repented.
True justice has compassion; false justice has only disdain.
And so the Lord went to the feasts of sinners,
like a good physician, who unless he touch the wound, frees no man from the disease.
Mystically: as Matthew after his conversion made Christ a feast in his house,
so each one converted to Christ ought to make him a spiritual feast
in his own spiritual house — that is, in his heart and conscience —
serving him with holy thoughts, meditations, and affections.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who in many ways callest and admonishest me to follow thee,
kindle my heart by thy divine inspiration,
that by thy grace I may follow thee who callest,
and that nothing may separate me from thy charity.
Grant that I may serve thee with holy thoughts, meditations, and affections,
and may set before thee a feast
from the great delight and devotion of my mind.
And thou who willest mercy rather than sacrifice,
and camest to call sinners rather than the just,
grant to me, a wretched sinner,
that I may deserve to know thy mercy mercifully shown in me. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Et vidit ibi, non tam corporis, quam mentis et miserantis intuitu, hominem.
latinJewel.english: And he saw there a man — not so much with the gaze of the body as with the gaze of the mind, and of one who has pity.
